name: MASP — Museu de Arte de São Paulo
description: Suspended 8 meters above Avenida Paulista by two massive red concrete beams, MASP creates a free public plaza beneath — a radical gesture of civic generosity. The glass-walled gallery above displays paintings on Lina's iconic crystal easels, liberating art from the wall.

images.alt: MASP suspended above Avenida Paulista with red beams

name: SESC Pompeia
description: A former drum factory transformed into a cultural and leisure center for São Paulo's working class. Two raw concrete towers connected by aerial walkways with irregular window openings rise above workshops, theaters, and a river-like swimming pool — every surface pulses with color and human activity.

name: Casa de Vidro
description: Lina Bo Bardi's first built work in Brazil and her own home — a glass box perched on slender pilotis above the Atlantic Forest canopy of Morumbi. The tropical vegetation gradually reclaimed the structure, blurring the boundary between architecture and nature in exactly the way she intended.
address: Rua General Almério de Moura, 200, São Paulo

images.alt: Casa de Vidro glass house surrounded by tropical forest

name: Teatro Oficina
description: A narrow, street-like theater space with exposed earth, scaffolding seating, and a retractable glass roof that rejects the traditional stage-audience divide. The audience becomes part of the performance in this raw, confrontational, and alive space that The Guardian called 'the world's best theatre.'

images.alt: Teatro Oficina interior with scaffolding and exposed earth

name: Solar do Unhão
description: A 16th-century sugar mill and slave quarters on the Bay of All Saints, transformed by Lina into the Museum of Modern Art of Bahia. The restoration preserved the colonial scars while inserting a striking wooden staircase inspired by local ox-cart wheel construction — popular craft elevated to architecture.

images.alt: Solar do Unhão colonial building overlooking the Bay of All Saints

name: 21st Century Museum of Contemporary Art, Kanazawa
description: A perfectly circular glass building with no front or back, inviting entry from every direction. SANAA's masterwork dissolves the boundary between museum and city through transparent walls and a constellation of white gallery boxes floating within an open, park-like plan.

images.alt: 21st Century Museum of Contemporary Art circular glass facade

name: Rolex Learning Center
description: A single undulating concrete slab that rises and dips like a landscape, creating hills, valleys, and patios within one continuous open floor. The building has no internal walls or doors — space is defined purely by topography, making movement through it feel like traversing a gentle terrain.

images.alt: Rolex Learning Center undulating concrete form on EPFL campus

name: New Museum
description: Seven stacked rectangular boxes, each slightly shifted off-axis, wrapped in an ethereal aluminum mesh skin. Rising from the gritty Bowery, the New Museum glows like a luminous lantern at night — SANAA's first building in North America and a quiet provocation against the surrounding urban density.

images.alt: New Museum stacked boxes on the Bowery

name: Louvre-Lens
description: A low, shimmering aluminum-clad pavilion set on the grounds of a former coal mine in northern France. The building's reflective surfaces mirror sky, trees, and visitors, making it almost disappear into its park — a democratic gesture that brings the Louvre's collection to a post-industrial community.

images.alt: Louvre-Lens reflective aluminum facade in parkland

name: Grace Farms
description: Known simply as 'The River,' this sinuous glass-and-steel structure winds down a hillside like a stream through a meadow. Five interconnected pavilions for sanctuary, community, culture, nature, and justice flow seamlessly into New England's rolling landscape — architecture as topography.
address: 365 Lukes Wood Road, New Canaan, CT 06840

images.alt: Grace Farms 'River' building winding through meadow landscape

name: Piscinas de Marés
description: Concrete walls gently guide the Atlantic Ocean into a series of tidal pools carved between the rocks of Leça da Palmeira. Siza's earliest masterwork is a lesson in restraint — the architecture barely announces itself, yet profoundly reshapes the experience of sea, sky, and stone.
address: Praia de Leça da Palmeira, Matosinhos, Portugal

images.alt: Piscinas de Marés concrete pools merging with Atlantic rocks

name: Serralves Museum
description: A luminous white volume set within the romantic gardens of the Serralves estate. The museum unfolds as a sequence of galleries connected by ramps and corridors that frame views of the surrounding park — Siza's characteristic integration of building, garden, and light at its most refined.
address: Rua Dom João de Castro, 210, Porto, Portugal

images.alt: Serralves Museum white facade amid park gardens

name: Faculty of Architecture, University of Porto
description: A campus of semi-circular and triangular pavilions cascading down a terraced hillside toward the Douro River. Each building dialogues with its neighbors through courtyards and covered passages, creating a village-like academic community where every turn reveals the river below.
address: Via Panorâmica Edgar Cardoso 215, Porto, Portugal

images.alt: Faculty of Architecture Porto pavilions overlooking Douro River

name: Portuguese Pavilion, Expo 98
description: Defined by a breathtaking catenary concrete canopy — a thin, draped sheet of reinforced concrete spanning 65 meters between two porticoes without any intermediate supports. The hovering veil creates a vast shaded ceremonial plaza that feels both impossibly light and anchored to the Tagus riverside.

images.alt: Portuguese Pavilion Expo 98 catenary concrete canopy

name: Iberê Camargo Foundation
description: A sculptural white concrete museum where looping ramps erupt from the main volume like arms reaching outward. Perched between a cliff and the Guaíba River in Porto Alegre, the building's dynamic form — Siza's most expressionistic work — creates a powerful dialogue between art, landscape, and movement.
address: Av. Padre Cacique, 2000, Porto Alegre, Brazil

images.alt: Iberê Camargo Foundation white concrete with looping ramps

name: Nový Dvůr Monastery
description: A Trappist monastery set in the Bohemian countryside where Pawson's radical minimalism meets the ascetic traditions of monastic life. Pristine white walls, precise proportions, and carefully calibrated natural light create spaces of extraordinary stillness — architecture stripped to its spiritual essence.

name: Design Museum London
description: A dramatic conversion of the 1960s Commonwealth Institute building, preserving its iconic hyperbolic paraboloid roof while inserting a luminous oak-lined atrium at its heart. Pawson's intervention transforms a Cold War-era landmark into a serene temple of design with three floors of flexible gallery space.

images.alt: Design Museum London oak-lined atrium under parabolic roof

name: Pawson House
description: John Pawson's own London home is the purest expression of his minimalist philosophy — a Victorian terraced house gutted and reborn as a sequence of calm, light-filled rooms where every surface, joint, and shadow is deliberate. The house became an icon of domestic minimalism and the subject of his book 'Living and Eating.'

name: Church of the Light
description: A cruciform slit carved into a bare concrete wall floods a darkened chapel with a cross of pure light. Tadao Ando's most iconic work reduces sacred architecture to its elemental essence — darkness, concrete, and the transcendent power of natural illumination.

name: Chichu Art Museum
description: Built almost entirely underground to preserve the landscape of Naoshima island, the museum uses only natural light to illuminate works by Monet, Turrell, and De Maria. Ando's subterranean concrete labyrinth transforms the act of viewing art into a meditative descent toward light.

name: Punta della Dogana
description: A 17th-century customs warehouse at the triangular tip where the Grand Canal meets the Giudecca Canal, restored by Ando as a contemporary art museum for the Pinault Collection. Raw concrete walls inserted within the historic brick shell create a powerful dialogue between Venice's maritime past and minimalist restraint.

name: 4×4 House
description: A slender four-story concrete tower occupying a mere four-by-four meter footprint on the Kobe waterfront. Each floor is a single room stacked vertically, with the top-floor living space framing panoramic views of the Akashi Kaikyo Bridge — an exercise in compressing domestic life to its vertical minimum.

name: Langen Foundation
description: Set within a former NATO missile base on Museum Island Hombroich, Ando's double-walled glass-and-concrete pavilion floats above a shallow reflecting pool. The austere galleries house Japanese and Western modern art in a space where military history and contemplative silence coexist.

name: Row House in Sumiyoshi
description: Ando's breakthrough work inserts a bare concrete box into the narrow slot of a traditional Osaka row house. An open courtyard bisects the plan, requiring residents to cross the outdoors to move between rooms — a radical assertion that architecture must engage nature, even at the cost of convenience.

name: Heydar Aliyev Center
description: A seamless white shell that flows from ground to roof without a single sharp edge, the Heydar Aliyev Center is Zaha Hadid's most fluid realization of her vision for architecture without corners. The building's undulating folds house a museum, gallery, and auditorium beneath a surface that seems to breathe.

name: MAXXI Museum
description: Rome's national museum of 21st-century art is a labyrinth of intersecting concrete walls and swooping staircases that defy orthogonal convention. Hadid's first major Italian commission won the Stirling Prize for its radical spatial complexity — visitors navigate overlapping circulation paths that create constantly shifting perspectives.

name: Guangzhou Opera House
description: Two pebble-like forms rest on the banks of the Pearl River, their faceted granite-and-glass surfaces evoking river-polished stones. Inside, a 1,800-seat auditorium unfolds in sweeping curves of smooth plaster, making Hadid's Guangzhou Opera House one of China's most acoustically and spatially ambitious cultural venues.
address: 1 Zhujiang West Road, Tianhe, Guangzhou, China

name: London Aquatics Centre
description: Designed for the 2012 Olympics, the Aquatics Centre's double-curved roof sweeps upward like a wave frozen in steel, spanning 160 meters over three pools. After the Games, the temporary wings were removed to reveal Hadid's original elegant parabolic form — a lasting legacy in London's Queen Elizabeth Olympic Park.
address: Queen Elizabeth Olympic Park, London E20 2ZQ

name: Vitra Fire Station
description: Hadid's first completed building — a series of sharp concrete planes that seem to be caught mid-explosion, freezing the urgency of a fire station into angular form. The radical deconstructivist composition announced a new architectural language that would define her career and influence a generation.

name: Therme Vals
description: Built from 60,000 slabs of locally quarried Valser quartzite, the thermal baths are carved into a mountainside like a cave. Zumthor orchestrates a primal sequence of light, steam, stone, and water — architecture experienced not through the eyes alone, but through the skin.

name: Kolumba Museum
description: Rising from the ruins of a Gothic church destroyed in World War II, Kolumba layers two thousand years of history into a single building. Zumthor's pale grey brick walls, perforated with a delicate filter of light, hover over Roman excavations, medieval fragments, and contemporary art in an act of profound architectural reconciliation.

name: Bruder Klaus Field Chapel
description: A rammed-earth tower standing alone in a farmer's field, its interior hollowed by burning 112 tree trunks to leave a charred, teardrop-shaped void open to the sky. Rain pools on the lead floor; light enters only from above and through tiny glass-plugged holes — a space of raw, almost primordial spirituality.

name: Kunsthaus Bregenz
description: A luminous glass-and-steel cube on the shores of Lake Constance, whose etched-glass facade diffuses Alpine daylight into pristine white gallery floors. Zumthor's deceptively simple envelope conceals a sophisticated system of light modulation — the building glows like a lantern, absorbing the colors of lake and sky.

name: Steilneset Memorial
description: A memorial to the 91 victims of the 17th-century Vardø witch trials, comprising Zumthor's smoked-glass pavilion housing a single burning chair by Louise Bourgeois, and a long timber-framed cocoon designed by the architect. Perched on the Arctic coast, the dual structures confront the violence of history with silence and flame.

name: Tate Modern
description: The transformation of Giles Gilbert Scott's Bankside Power Station into the world's most visited modern art museum redefined what adaptive reuse could achieve. Herzog & de Meuron's masterstroke was restraint — the vast Turbine Hall became a cathedral-scale entrance, and the raw industrial character was preserved rather than polished away.

name: Elbphilharmonie
description: A glittering glass wave perched atop a massive brick warehouse on the Elbe River, the Elbphilharmonie fuses Hamburg's maritime heritage with 21st-century acoustical engineering. The concert hall within, sculpted by algorithms to optimize sound, seats 2,100 listeners in a terraced vineyard configuration of extraordinary intimacy.
address: Platz der Deutschen Einheit 1, 20457 Hamburg, Germany

name: Bird's Nest (Beijing National Stadium)
description: A lattice of interwoven steel beams wraps a 91,000-seat stadium in what appears to be a colossal bird's nest — structure and ornament fused into a single gesture. Designed for the 2008 Olympics with Ai Weiwei as artistic consultant, it became an instant icon of China's emergence on the world stage.
address: 1 National Stadium S Rd, Chaoyang, Beijing, China

name: CaixaForum Madrid
description: A former power station seemingly levitated off the ground by removing its granite base and inserting a rusted Corten steel crown, CaixaForum creates the illusion of a massive building floating above the Paseo del Prado. Patrick Blanc's vertical garden on the adjacent wall completes a provocative dialogue between architecture and nature.

name: Pérez Art Museum Miami
description: Elevated on stilts above Biscayne Bay to address both sea-level rise and hurricane flooding, PAMM is wrapped in hanging gardens and deep wooden verandas that blur the line between indoor and outdoor. Herzog & de Meuron's tropical museum redefines the relationship between art institution and subtropical climate.

name: 1111 Lincoln Road
description: A parking garage elevated to the status of architecture — each floor has a different height, and the open concrete structure doubles as an event venue, retail space, and rooftop residence. Herzog & de Meuron proved that even the most mundane program can become extraordinary when treated with spatial ambition.

name: Centre Pompidou
description: Co-designed with Richard Rogers, the Centre Pompidou turned architecture inside out — exposing its structural skeleton, mechanical systems, and circulation in a riot of color-coded pipes and escalator tubes on the facade. The radical high-tech provocation scandalized Paris and democratized the museum, drawing millions to its open piazza.

name: The Shard
description: At 310 meters, The Shard is Western Europe's tallest building — a crystalline spire of glass shards that tapers to an open apex, deliberately left incomplete as if dissolving into London's sky. Piano described it as a 16th-century pinnacle; critics called it a shard of glass driven into the heart of historic London.

name: Whitney Museum of American Art
description: Anchoring the southern end of the High Line, the Whitney's asymmetric stack of cantilevered galleries steps outward as it rises, creating expansive outdoor terraces with views of the Hudson River. Piano's industrial-chic vocabulary of steel, glass, and raw concrete pays homage to the Meatpacking District's working-class roots.

name: Fondation Beyeler
description: A long, low pavilion of red porphyry walls and an overhanging glass roof set at the edge of a water garden in Riehen. Piano's design treats the museum as a finely tuned instrument for viewing art in natural light — the thin roof plane appears to float, and every gallery opens to views of the park and distant Vosges mountains.

name: California Academy of Sciences
description: A living green roof of native California plants undulates over an aquarium, planetarium, and natural history museum in Golden Gate Park. Piano's design is one of the greenest museums on earth — the roof's rolling hills echo the surrounding landscape, and skylights open automatically to ventilate the building naturally.
address: 55 Music Concourse Drive, San Francisco, CA 94118

name: Villa Savoye
description: The manifesto of Le Corbusier's Five Points of Architecture made real — pilotis lift a white box above a meadow, a roof garden replaces the ground, ribbon windows wrap the facade, and a free plan liberates the interior. Villa Savoye remains the most influential house of the 20th century, a machine for living in that is also a poem.

name: Notre-Dame du Haut (Ronchamp)
description: Perched on a hilltop in the Vosges foothills, Ronchamp's swooping concrete roof and thick, light-punctured walls shattered every expectation of what Le Corbusier — and modern architecture — could be. The chapel is sculptural, primal, and deeply spiritual, its irregular windows scattering colored light like a constellation across rough whitewashed walls.
address: 13 Rue de la Chapelle, 70250 Ronchamp, France

name: Unité d'Habitation
description: A self-contained 'vertical village' for 1,600 residents, raised on massive pilotis with internal streets, shops, a school, and a rooftop gymnasium. The Unité's raw béton brut finish — board-marked concrete left unfinished — gave birth to Brutalism, and its social ambition remains the benchmark for collective housing.

name: Sainte Marie de La Tourette
description: A Dominican monastery hovering on pilotis above a sloping meadow near Lyon, its raw concrete corridors, cells, and chapel enclose a stark cloister open to the sky. La Tourette is Le Corbusier's most uncompromising exploration of light, proportion, and monastic austerity — an architecture that demands contemplation.
address: Route de la Tourette, 69210 Éveux, France

name: Carpenter Center for the Visual Arts
description: Le Corbusier's only building in North America threads a curving ramp through a concrete volume on the Harvard campus, inviting passersby to walk through the building itself. The brise-soleil, pilotis, and free-form plan demonstrate every principle of his architecture — a built manifesto set incongruously among Georgian brick.

name: Chandigarh Capitol Complex
description: The governmental heart of Le Corbusier's planned city of Chandigarh — comprising the High Court, Secretariat, and Legislative Assembly — is a monumental composition of raw concrete set against the Himalayan foothills. A UNESCO World Heritage Site, the Capitol Complex represents the most ambitious realization of modernist urban planning on a civic scale.
